📘 EXAM PREPARATION STRATEGIES
🔶 Midterm Exam Preparation Strategy

Begin preparation 2–3 weeks before midterm exams. Create a detailed study schedule covering all subjects with dedicated time slots. Review lecture notes, textbooks, and instructor-provided supplementary materials. Practice previous years’ question papers and sample problems (available in library archives). Form study groups of 3–4 classmates for collaborative learning and concept clarification. Attend professor and TA review sessions and utilize office hours. Use the Pomodoro technique: 25 minutes study + 5 minutes break. Sleep 7–8 hours a night, especially during exam week. Stay hydrated and eat healthy meals. What to Prioritize

Focus on syllabus topics with higher weightage. Review assignments and quizzes where marks were lost. Understand concepts rather than memorizing. Last-Week Tips

One week before exams, stop exploring new topics. Focus on revision and practice. Prepare quick-review notes or flashcards. Solve timed mock tests to improve speed and accuracy. 🔶 End-Semester Exam Strategy

Begin preparing 4–5 weeks before end-semester exams. End-semester exams cover the entire syllabus, so planning is crucial. Create a master study timeline:

First 2–3 weeks: Deep conceptual study

Next 1–2 weeks: Intensive practice, problem-solving

Final week: Revision + mock tests

Weightage Allocation

Allocate time based on:

Course credits

Topics you are weak in

Difficulty level

📚 SUBJECT-SPECIFIC STUDY TIPS
🧮 Mathematics & Engineering Subjects

Practice heavily: Solve 50–100 problems per topic. Understand derivations and proofs. Maintain formula sheets for quick revision. 💻 Programming Courses

Write code daily. Practice debugging. Understand time and space complexity. Solve competitive coding problems regularly. 📖 Theory Subjects

Make structured notes with headings/subheadings. Use diagrams, charts, and flowcharts for clarity. Create 1–2 page summaries for each chapter. 🔬 Laboratory Courses

Understand both procedures and underlying theory. Review lab manuals and your observation records. Be ready to explain experiment results during viva. 🏛️ STUDY RESOURCES & ACADEMIC SUPPORT

🕒 TIME MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES
🗓️ Daily Routine

Attend all classes and labs. Spend 2–3 hours daily on self-study. Revise the day’s lectures the same evening. 📅 Weekend Planning

Use weekends to:

Catch up on pending assignments

Study difficult topics in depth

Work on projects

📝 Assignment Management

Start assignments as soon as they’re announced. Break large work into small tasks with mini-deadlines. Seek help from peers, TAs, or professors well before the deadline.

Seek help from peers, TAs, or professors well before the deadline. ⛔ Avoiding Procrastination

Use focus apps like Forest / Pomodoro timers. Keep your phone silent or in another room. Set specific daily goals. Reward yourself after meeting targets. Study in library or designated study spaces. 📝 EXAM DAY PREPARATION
🌙 Night Before Exam

Do:

Light revision of key formulas and concepts

Prepare pens, calculator, ID card, admit card

Set multiple alarms

Sleep 7 hours minimum

Don’t:

Don’t start new topics

Avoid heavy study after 9 PM

Avoid excess caffeine

Don’t join stressful conversations

📅 On Exam Day
Morning Routine

Wake up at least 2 hours early

Eat a good breakfast

Carry water/snacks

Reach venue 20–30 minutes early

During Exam

Read instructions carefully

Attempt easy questions first

Manage time using mark-based allocation

Leave last few minutes for review

If You Feel Stuck

Skip the question and come back later

Focus on maximizing overall marks

Department: CSE
Prerequisites: None
Description: Basics of algorithms, problem solving, and programming logic using Python. CS101 – Foundations of Programming (4 credits)
Fundamentals of programming, control structures, functions, and data types. CS102 – Discrete Mathematics (3 credits)
Logic, sets, relations, combinatorics, graphs, and fundamental proof techniques. CS110 – Digital Logic and Systems (3 credits)
Binary systems, logic gates, Boolean algebra, and combinational circuits. CS111 – Engineering Mathematics I (3 credits)
Covers calculus, differential equations, and linear algebra basics. Semester 2

Covers OOP principles, classes, inheritance, and design patterns. CS121 – Data Structures (4 credits)

CPU architecture, memory hierarchy, instruction sets, and assembly basics. CS130 – Software Engineering I (3 credits)
Prerequisite: CS101
SDLC, UML, requirements analysis, and introductory testing. CS131 – Probability & Statistics for CS (3 credits)

Prerequisite: CS122

Prerequisite: CS202
Distributed computing, consensus algorithms, fault tolerance, and scalability. CS301 – Compiler Design (4 credits)

Prerequisite: CS210
Storage engines, query optimizations, indexing, and transactions. CS310 – Advanced Networks (3 credits)
Prerequisite: CS211

Prerequisite: CS202
Cryptography basics, secure coding, threat modeling, privacy. CS331 – Mobile App Development (3 credits)
Prerequisite: CS230
Frameworks, offline-first design, platform-specific optimizations. Semester 7

Project proposal, planning, initial implementation. CS401 – Elective: Advanced Algorithms (3 credits)
Prerequisite: CS220

Text processing, embeddings, sequence models.

🧪 UNIVERSITY LAB SAFETY GUIDELINES (RAG-FRIENDLY FORMAT)

Academic Year: 2025–2026
Purpose: To promote safe working practices in all engineering and science laboratories. Principle: Safety first — no experiment may be performed without supervision and proper protective equipment. 🔰 GENERAL LAB SAFETY RULES

Lab coats, closed shoes, and ID cards must be worn at all times. Eating, drinking, and chewing gum inside laboratories is prohibited. Mobile phones must remain on silent and may only be used with permission. Follow all instructions given by laboratory instructors and staff. Report unsafe behavior, spills, or damaged equipment immediately to the lab assistant. Know the location of emergency exits, fire extinguishers, and first-aid kits. Do not conduct experiments without explicit permission. Running, horseplay, and loud talking are strictly forbidden. 🥽 PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMENT (PPE)
Mandatory in All Laboratories

Closed-toe shoes

Lab coat or apron

Safety goggles (where required)

University ID card

Additional PPE for Specific Labs

Mechanical Engineering Lab:

Safety shoes

Gloves

Face shield for machining

Chemical Lab:

Chemical-resistant gloves

Face mask

Safety goggles

Electronics Lab:

Antistatic (ESD) wrist strap

ESD-safe footwear

Robotics Lab:

Gloves

Safety shoes

Workshop:

Ear protection

Safety helmet

⚙️ MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB SAFETY
Hazards

Rotating machinery

High-temperature surfaces

Sharp tools

Compressed gas cylinders

Rules

Tie long hair and avoid loose clothing near machines. Do not operate machines without supervision. Ensure machine guards and shields are in place. Use tongs or insulated gloves to handle hot materials. Remove metal chips with brushes, not bare hands. Emergency Procedures

Use emergency stop button to shut off machines. Use a fire blanket for grease fires. Report all injuries immediately. 💻 COMPUTER LAB SAFETY
Hazards

Loose wires and tripping risks

Overheating computers

Static discharge

Cable clutter

Rules

Do not connect unauthorized USB drives. Installation of software is not allowed without lab permission. No food or drinks near computers. Save work frequently and properly log out. Use ESD wrist straps when working with hardware components. Cyber Safety Rules

Do not access restricted servers or private networks. Use only licensed software. Follow university network usage policies. 🤖 AI & DATA SCIENCE LAB SAFETY
Hazards

High-power GPU systems

Overheating compute servers

Electrical overloads

Sensitive data environments

Rules

Ensure proper ventilation for GPU units. Never modify server hardware. Handle datasets securely and follow privacy regulations. Avoid plugging multiple high-wattage devices into a single extension board. Data Ethics Guidelines

Use datasets only for approved academic work. Do not store sensitive personal data without authorization. Follow fairness, transparency, and responsible AI principles. 🔌 ELECTRONICS LAB SAFETY
Hazards

Hot soldering irons

High-voltage circuits

Capacitor discharge

Static-sensitive ICs

Rules

Always turn off power before adjusting wiring. Use fume extractors when soldering. Wear ESD straps while handling chips. Discharge capacitors before touching circuits. Do not work on live circuits without supervision. Emergency Procedures

Turn off main power supply in case of short circuit. Use CO₂ fire extinguishers for electrical fires. ⚗️ CHEMISTRY LAB SAFETY
Hazards

Corrosive chemicals

Flammable substances

Toxic fumes

Glassware breakage

Rules

Always wear gloves and goggles when handling chemicals. Work inside a fume hood for volatile chemicals. Label all reagent bottles clearly. Dispose chemical waste only in designated containers. Never pipette chemicals by mouth—use pipette fillers. First Aid Procedures

For chemical spills on skin: rinse under water for 15 minutes. For chemical eye exposure: use eyewash station immediately. All accidents must be reported to the instructor. 🔭 PHYSICS LAB SAFETY
Hazards

Laser beams

High-voltage power supplies

Optical equipment

Cryogenic materials

Rules

Never look directly into lasers or reflections. Secure optical components to prevent falls. Handle cryogens with insulated gloves. Follow voltage safety guidelines strictly. 🤖 ROBOTICS & IOT LAB SAFETY
Hazards

Moving robotic arms

High-current motors

Battery explosions

Sharp mechanical edges

Rules

Maintain a safe distance from robotic mechanisms. Turn off power before wiring microcontrollers. Do not overcharge lithium-ion batteries. Check motor driver ratings before use. Equipment Guidelines

Proper grounding of robotics workstations is mandatory. Emergency stop buttons must be accessible. Secure all loose wires to avoid entanglement.

Secure all loose wires to avoid entanglement. 🔧 WORKSHOP & MANUFACTURING SAFETY
Hazards

Grinding sparks

Welding arc flashes

Rotating machinery

Cutting tools

Rules

Wear welding masks during welding operations. Safety shoes and gloves required at all times. Never leave machines running unattended. Clamp workpieces properly before machining. Equipment Use

Only use angle grinders with protective guards. Wear ear protection near noisy machines. Report malfunctioning equipment immediately. ⚠️ HAZARD CLASSIFICATION

Low Risk: Computer Labs, AI Labs (non-hardware tasks)

Medium Risk: Electronics Lab, Physics Lab

High Risk: Mechanical Lab, Workshop, Chemical Lab, Robotics Lab

🚨 INCIDENT REPORTING PROCEDURE

Steps to report lab incidents:

Inform the lab assistant immediately. Fill the incident form on the Lab Safety Portal. If injured, visit the campus medical center. Severe incidents will be reported to the Department Safety Committee. Emergency Contacts

This handbook outlines academic rules, attendance requirements, exam regulations, conduct expectations, digital behavior, anti-ragging laws, disciplinary actions, and student support policies. 🎓 ATTENDANCE POLICY

Minimum attendance requirement: 75% in each course. Attendance shortage rules:

65% to 75%: Allowed only with medical certificate or approved leave. Below 65%: Student is not allowed to appear in exams under any circumstances. Medical leave procedure:

Submit a medical certificate within 7 days of returning to campus. Upload through the Academic Portal. Approval from academic committee required. Attendance notes:

Lab and theory attendance are calculated separately. Students entering more than 10 minutes late are marked absent. Attendance is updated weekly. 📝 GRADING SYSTEM
Grade Scale

O: Outstanding (90–100)

A+: Excellent (80–89)

A: Very Good (70–79)

B+: Good (60–69)

B: Above Average (50–59)

C: Average (40–49)

F: Fail (<40)

I: Incomplete

W: Withdrawn

SGPA / CGPA Rules

SGPA = Σ(Credit × GradePoint) / Σ(Credits)

CGPA = Weighted average of all SGPAs. Improvement policy: Only F-grade students can repeat a course. 🧾 EXAM RULES AND REGULATIONS

University ID card is mandatory during exams. Allowed items: pens, pencils, erasers, transparent bottle, non-programmable calculator. Prohibited items: mobile phones, smart watches, Bluetooth devices, unauthorized notes. Late entry rules:

Up to 15 minutes: entry allowed. After 15 minutes: not permitted. Malpractice policy:

First offense → Semester suspension + zero marks. Repeat offense → Permanent debarment. Answer Sheet Rules

Rough work only on last pages. Do not tear pages. Write ID clearly on top. Exam Revaluation

Window: 14 days after results. Fee: ₹1000 per subject. Marks can increase, decrease, or remain same. 📚 ACADEMIC INTEGRITY
Plagiarism Policy

Allowed similarity: Below 15%. Tools used: Turnitin, URKUND. Actions:

15–30% → Rewrite with penalty. 30% → Zero marks + disciplinary notice. Repeated cases → Semester suspension. Collaboration Rules

Group assignments: only within assigned groups. Individual work must not be shared. AI-generated content must be cited. 🧑‍⚖️ CONDUCT RULES
General Code of Conduct

Maintain discipline. No disruptive behavior in class. Respect faculty, staff, and students. Abusive language is prohibited. Dress Code

Regular days: Smart casuals recommended. Lab days: Shoes and lab coat mandatory. Formal events: Formal attire required. Classroom Rules

Phones on silent. Recording requires permission. Eating in class is not allowed. 🚫 ANTI-RAGGING POLICY

Zero-tolerance as per UGC Act. Examples of ragging include verbal abuse, humiliation, forcing acts, isolation, bullying, social media harassment. Punishments:
